name: Parallel Codegen Units
summary: Split the program into many independent codegen units; compile each in parallel. rustc and LLVM both use this approach. Trade-off: more parallelism vs lost cross-unit inlining. ThinLTO recovers most of the cross-unit optimisation while keeping the parallelism.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Modern compilers split the program into many independent **codegen units** (CGUs) and compile each in parallel. rustc partitions a crate into 16 CGUs by default (release builds); LLVM's parallel codegen splits modules similarly.

The approach is straightforward: for each function, decide which CGU it belongs to (usually based on a hash of the function name plus some load-balancing); within each CGU, run the optimiser and codegen normally; link the CGUs at the end. The wall-clock time scales near-linearly with cores up to the CGU count.

sections.title: Trade-offs
sections.content:
The trade-off is **lost cross-unit inlining**. Functions that would have been inlined across CGU boundaries by a single-CGU compile aren't inlined when split across CGUs. The performance impact is workload-dependent: ~5–15% on Rust release builds typically.

ThinLTO is the modern answer to recover most of the cross-unit optimisation while keeping the parallelism. By exporting per-CGU summaries and making globally-aware inlining decisions in the linker, ThinLTO captures most of Full LTO's wins at near-CGU-parallel build speed.

For a new language, the question is at what CGU granularity to default. Too few CGUs: parallelism is wasted. Too many CGUs: cross-unit inlining loss dominates. rustc's default of 16 is a reasonable starting point but workload-dependent.
